content.problemStatement:
Tzuyu gave Nayeon a strip of NNN cells (numbered 111 through NNN) for her birthday. This strip is described by a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, where for each valid iii, the iii-th cell is blocked if Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1 or free if Ai=0A_i = 0Ai​=0. Tzuyu and Nayeon are going to use it to play a game with the following rules:


The players alternate turns; Nayeon plays first.
Initially, both players are outside of the strip. However, note that afterwards during the game, their positions are always different.
In each turn, the current player should choose a free cell and move there. Afterwards, this cell becomes blocked and the players cannot move to it again.
If it is the current player's first turn, she may move to any free cell.
Otherwise, she may only move to one of the left and right adjacent cells, i.e. from a cell ccc, the current player may only move to the cell c−1c-1c−1 or c+1c+1c+1 (if it is free).
If a player is unable to move to a free cell during her turn, this player loses the game.
Nayeon and Tzuyu are very smart, so they both play optimally. Since it is Nayeon's birthday, she wants to know if she can beat Tzuyu. Find out who wins.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 3·10^5). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN where Ai is 0 (free) or 1 (blocked) and A1 = AN = 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if the first player (Nayeon) wins with optimal play, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case: longest zero segment length is 3 (odd) and second longest is 0, (3+1)/2=2>0 → Yes. Second case: longest length is 2 (even) → No. Third case: only one zero, length 1 (odd) and second longest 0, (1+1)/2=1>0 → Yes. Fourth case: no zeros → No.

solution.approach: The strip consists of blocks of consecutive free cells (0s) separated by blocked cells (1s). After a player’s first move she is confined to the block where she started, because later moves are only to adjacent free cells. Thus each player will consume cells inside a single zero‑segment. The optimal first move is to pick the middle cell of the longest segment. If that segment length L is even, the opponent can always mirror the move and win, so the first player loses. If L is odd, the segment is split into two equal halves of length (L‑1)/2. After the first move the largest remaining free segment has length max( (L‑1)/2 , secondLargest ), where secondLargest is the length of the second longest original segment. The first player wins exactly when (L‑1)/2 is strictly larger than secondLargest, i.e. when L is odd and (L+1)/2 > secondLargest. Therefore the algorithm is: 1) Scan the array and collect lengths of all zero‑segments. 2) Find the largest (maxLen) and second largest (secondLen) lengths. 3) Answer "Yes" if maxLen is odd and (maxLen+1)/2 > secondLen, otherwise "No".
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(∑N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
